title: Speculative tool-schema hydration causing delayed streamed chunk delivery and mid-stream timeouts
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports sharp increase in end-to-end latency for streamed responses when prompts include tool calls. The stream starts but streamed chunks are delayed ~2-8s mid-response, occasionally leading to client-side timeouts.

Impact: Affects multiple production customers (enterprise tier). Intermittent but reproducible with tool call enabled; increases tail latency and causes streaming sessions to abort after client-side timeouts.

Environment: prod cluster in us-east, streaming route /v1/chat/stream with toolcall preflight enabled and schema-hydration flag set to speculative=true. Observed for rw-open-7b-v1 and rw-llama-13b-v2.

Observed behavior: Initial tokens stream normally for ~200-400ms, then a stall of 2-8s while server emits heartbeats but no content chunks. Traces show a synchronous preflight/schema hydration step invoked mid-stream for tool metadata.

Expected behavior: Preflight/schema validation should either complete before the first chunk is sent or be performed asynchronously without blocking chunk emission. Streaming should not stall due to synchronous schema hydration after initial token emission.

title: Staggered autoscale window: transient GPU attrition + noisy-tenant correlated throughput collapse
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports large p99/p50 throughput collapse during the daily autoscale window (09:00-09:12 UTC) for their dedicated pool. Impact: production request latency p99 jumped from ~700ms to 6–12s, and sustained token throughput dropped by ~55% for ~8 minutes.

Impact:
- Customer-facing inference frontends experienced long tail latencies and 5xx spikes (mostly 502/504).
- Several requests timed out causing downstream job failures in ScribeAI pipelines.
- Business impact: queued jobs and delayed processing for ~350k inference tokens.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool id: dp-78a4 (ScribeAI reserved cluster)
- Region: us-east-1
- Serving runtime version: v2.18.3
- Autoscaler version: v1.9.1
- Model variants: redwood/gpt-4o-mini-v1 pinned at 2026-03-10

Steps to reproduce (customer provided):
1) Send steady QPS ~25 with mixed short/long streams between 08:50-09:10 UTC.
2) Wait for autoscale window at 09:00 UTC (predictive increase expected).
3) Observe sudden p99 and throughput collapse between 09:02–09:10 and intermittent 502s.

Observed behavior:
- Autoscaler detached 2 GPU nodes in staggered fashion (09:01:30 and 09:02:10) while queue depth briefly rose.
- Several GPU attachments failed with NVLink handshake WARNs in node logs and one driver reset observed (dmesg recorded nvidia-mig reset).
- A noisy tenant in the same reserved pool produced many short-lived high-frequency multiplexed streams at the same time (traffic spike on tenant-id t-1946).

Expected behavior:
- Autoscaler should avoid removing capacity mid-window when queue depth is above the hot threshold and should not induce driver resets.

Logs/metrics provided:
- link to customer dashboard (redacted): https://console.redwood.ai/dp-78a4/metrics?from=2026-03-12T08:30&to=2026-03-12T09:30
- kernel/driver excerpt attached by customer (snippet included in comments).

title: Intermittent generation requests with very large max_tokens time out near token-window boundary (KV-cache refill stall suspected)
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports intermittent 504 / request timeouts for single generation requests when using very large max_tokens (typically >= 120k token window). Failures occur late in the generation (after ~70-95% of requested tokens produced). Suspected stall during KV-cache refill / eviction cycle in serving runtime when prefill and decode phases overlap under heavy batching.

Impact:
- Affects multiple request attempts from NimbusAI production workloads on Dedicated pools in us-east; blocking their long-transcript generation features.
- Symptoms: spike in 5xx (504 gateway timeouts) and client-observed disconnects; partial outputs received before termination.

Environment:
- Dedicated customer pool (dedicated), prod:us-east; models: gptx-16b-fp16 primarily.
- Requests routed through API gateway with default 60s client timeout in their code, but server-side timeouts observed beyond that.

Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) POST /v1/generate using model gptx-16b-fp16 on dedicated pool.
2) Set prompt ~40k tokens, max_tokens=120000, stream=false, temperature=0.2.
3) Send concurrently from 6 clients to simulate production load.
4) Observe intermittent 504 from API gateway after long-running generation (often after ~30-50k tokens produced).

Observed behavior:
- Request occasionally returns 504 with no finalizing token. Partial stream of tokens earlier in the response.
- Serving runtime logs show long stall in "kv_cache_refill" and spikes in kernel scheduling latency.

Expected behavior:
- Long-context generation should continue to completion (or return a graceful error with a helpful hint) rather than sudden 504 mid-generation.

Customer-provided artifacts:
- Trace IDs: trace-us-east-78a4a3b, trace-us-east-78a4a3c (attached to ticket).
- Client-side logs showing request timings and partial payloads (attached).

Notes: This differs from previously reported "streaming hang" tickets because the requests do return partial tokens before failing and failure appears correlated to internal KV cache operations when requests are near the model token-window boundary.

title: Mid-cycle seat add led to prorated surcharge removing negotiated volume discount on consolidated invoice
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer SiftMail reports a charge on their March consolidated invoice that appears to negate their negotiated volume discount after adding 12 seats mid-cycle. The invoice shows a prorated "surcharge" line where per-seat pricing reverted to list price for the prorated portion rather than applying the customer's tiered volume discount.

Impact:
- Customer sees a $4,322 unexpected line item affecting month close and internal chargebacks.
- Customer concerned this will recur on future mid-cycle seat changes.

Environment:
- Enterprise org on reserved/dedicated plan with negotiated tier: 31+ seats => $1.80/seat; 11-30 seats => $2.10/seat.
- New seats added 2026-03-05T15:40:12Z (12 seats) via admin console. Billing cycle starts 1st of month.

Requested outcome:
- Clarify why prorate used list price for added seats instead of applying the negotiated tier once total seat count exceeded threshold mid-cycle.
- Provide provisional credit for the overcharge and confirm permanent fix to pricing-engine logic for mid-cycle seat tiering.

title: Intermittent 502/503 errors during large embedding uploads due to chunked body aborts at API gateway
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer LexiconSoft reports elevated 502/503 errors and failed embedding upload jobs when sending large batched embedding payloads (~50–200MB) using our REST API over HTTP/2 with chunked transfer encoding. Errors began 2026-03-12 09:10 UTC after a gradual traffic increase.

Impact:
- Several customer embedding jobs failed (25% failure rate on a 40-job run).
- Increased API error-rate across their tenant (~P50->P95 error increase).
- Affects production P95 latency and caused retry storms from client side.

Environment:
- prod cluster in us-east-1; customer on enterprise plan with Dedicated routing to high-priority pool.
- Requests use HTTP/2 with chunked transfer-encoding and per-request streaming.
- Model: text-embedding-3-large pinned for this customer.

Observed behavior and tentative root cause hypothesis:
- API gateway logs show upstream proxy closing the request stream with 502 when the client finishes chunked body. Runtime logs show the runtime process logging: "ClientRequest aborted while reading request body" immediately followed by a worker restart in a subset of pods.
- Hypothesis: a mismatch in keepalive/read-timeout handling between apigw and runtime-proxy for large chunked uploads leads to the runtime aborting body read, causing the gateway to surface a 502. The abort path is triggering an upstream crash or worker restart under memory pressure when multiple concurrent large uploads coincide.

Steps to reproduce (internal):
1) Use a test client to POST 10 embedding batches of 1000 items each, chunked over HTTP/2 with ~10MB chunk sizes.
2) Target the LexiconSoft tenant route and run at concurrency 8 from 3 clients to simulate their sustained upload.
3) Observe apigw responses and runtime stderr. Reproduce reliably in ~4/10 runs in our staging cluster when configured with low keepalive (10s) and request read timeout of 30s.

Logs and metrics (samples):
- apigw: request_id=apigw-04b2c, upstream_status=502, upstream_message="upstream read aborted" (2026-03-12T09:17:32Z)
- runtime: [worker-7] ERROR: ClientRequest aborted during request body read (stack trace attached in ticket artifacts).
- k8s: OOM kill count on runtime pod remained 0, but rss spiked to 78% of memory before restart.
- traces: span shows request body finish event occurs after runtime has flagged EOF read timeout.

Temporary workaround provided to customer:
- Advise LexiconSoft to reduce chunk sizes to <2MB and use lower concurrency for bulk uploads. This reduces observed failures while we investigate. Customer accepted and confirmed temporary workaround on 2026-03-12 18:00 UTC.

Investigation notes:
- SRE reproduced the abort locally when apigw keepalive timeout set to 8–12s and runtime request-read timeout at 30s; extending the runtime read timeout to 120s eliminated the abort in repro.
- The runtime code path for chunked body parsing does not gracefully handle mid-stream connection resets and triggers an assert followed by an emergency worker restart in a race condition.
- The issue is amplified when multiple large uploads hit the same worker and memory footprint increases; while not OOM, the worker restarts during memory compaction cycles.
- No evidence of keystore/tls rotation involvement in initial repro.

Proposed immediate fixes:
- Backport hotfix to runtime-proxy to tolerate early client-side resets and return a 499-style response to apigw instead of triggering a worker restart. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/apigw/pull/2248 (in review).
- Adjust default request-read timeout in apigw->runtime route to 120s for large-upload routes and add a per-tenant override for enterprise customers.
- Add guardrails in runtime to avoid restart on client-abort during body read; convert assert to soft-fail with controlled resource cleanup.

Longer-term actions:
- Add end-to-end load test scenario for chunked/streamed large payloads and include in canary gating.
- Improve memory accounting for streaming workers and add an alert when rss > 70% sustained during large-upload windows.

Expected next steps and owners:
- SRE (Miguel Ortega): finish runtime hotfix review and deploy to canary by 2026-03-15.
- Runtime eng (Priya Ramesh): change assert-to-soft-fail, prepare postmortem draft.
- Support (Ava Chen): coordinate with LexiconSoft for gradual rollout and confirm fix impact.

Artifacts attached: stack traces (logs/stacktrace-221111.txt), apigw traces (traces/apigw-04b2c.json), reproduction script (scripts/repro_chunked_upload.sh).

title: Concurrent client retries split transaction boundary causing duplicate tool executions across Python and Go SDKs
preview: Issue summary: Customer reports duplicated external side effects (webhook POST to order processing) when their app issues concurrent retries from both Python (2.4.1) and Go (1.7.0) SDK clients in a short window. Impact: Duplicate orders reaching downstream systems, billing exposure, and customer trust concern. Observed behavior: Under burst failure conditions the orchestrator appears to split what the client treats as a single transactional interaction into two distinct server-side request groups, and two tool invocations are delivered to the downstream webhook. Expected behavior: A single external tool call (webhook) per logical user action even if the client retries locally; SDK/platform should provide clear idempotency guarantee guidance and mechanisms to prevent duplicate side-effects.

title: TCP timestamp stripping by corporate firewall causes intermittent gRPC reconnects and degraded streaming in private deployment
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers on a private VPC deployment intermittently observe gRPC stream resets and frequent reconnects when sending long-lived streaming inference requests. Impact: streaming clients experience token duplication, reconnect churn, and increased tail latency, causing transient user-facing failures during payments reconciliation jobs.

Impact:
- Multiple customers reported reconnect storms during peak batch inference windows (FinNova observed ~8% request failures, clients retried and duplicated outputs).
- Tail latency increases from 120ms median to >1.5s p95 for streaming responses.

Environment:
- Deployment: Private (customer-managed VPC peering + egress through corporate firewall)
- Region: us-east-1 (customer-routed egress)
- Redwood runtime: serving-runtime v2026.02.10
- OBS: TLS stays up; application-level gRPC shows RST and transient EOFs.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Customer runs a long-lived streaming generation job (streaming chat with >1024 token context) against a pinned redwood-7b-v2 model on their private deployment.
2) Load generator runs 100 parallel streams per node for ~30 minutes.
3) Observe intermittent request resets and reconnects in client logs; server sees TCP-level retransmits and occasional out-of-order segments.

Observed behavior:
- gRPC status CANCELLED / UNAVAILABLE on clients with reconnects multiple times per minute.
- Kernel/tcpdump on serving node shows TCP timestamp fields stripped or zeroed on some packets once flow exits through corporate firewall.
- When timestamps are stripped, TCP duplicate-detection heuristics mis-evaluate sequence numbers under reordering, leading to spurious retransmits and head-of-line blocking.

Expected behavior:
- No mid-path modification of TCP options (timestamps) for established flows; stable long-lived gRPC streams with graceful keep-alives.

Logs/Traces:
- Attached extract (sanitized) from serving node tcpdump: repeated TCP options length mismatch and TSval=0 in packets after 2026-03-12T15:21:10Z.
- Stackdriver/Datadog spans: multiple grpc:stream closed events correlated with increased retransmit counters on node NIC.

Temporary workaround:
- Customer routed egress around corporate firewall for a subset of serving nodes (temporary egress NAT through a managed firewall) and saw immediate reduction in reconnects.
- Short-term mitigation: reduce concurrent streams per node and enable smaller keepalive intervals to force faster recovery while we investigate.

Next steps:
- Coordinate with customer networking to capture full pcap across egress path and corporate firewall appliance.
- Reproduce in controlled staging with a middlebox that strips TCP timestamps.
- Evaluate runtime-level mitigation (disabling reliance on RFC1323 TS for duplicate detection in our user-space TCP stack or tuning kernel parameters) and a longer-term recommendation for corporate firewall config (preserve TCP options).

title: Main-thread GC starvation in session-sidecar causing cascading 503s and api-gateway retries
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers observed a sudden rise in 5xx responses (primarily 503 / upstream unavailable) from the hosted API in us-west during high-concurrency streaming workloads. Error wave started 2026-03-13 02:12 UTC and lasted ~45 minutes with intermittent recovery windows.

Impact:
- LexiDocs (dedicated tenant) reported ~35% of streaming sessions failing with 503s and mid-request disconnects.
- Platform-wide p99 tail latency for text-generation increased from 110ms -> 1.8s for affected hosts.
- API gateway showed elevated retry rates causing localized threadpool exhaustion.

Environment:
prod/us-west, dedicated pool for LexiDocs. Session-sidecars (Python 3.11) present on each node to manage streaming sessions and multipart uploads. Serving runtime (Rust) remained mostly healthy on GPU hosts.

Timeline / Observed signals:
- 02:10 UTC: LexiDocs bulk streaming load begins (concurrent sessions spike + long-lived streaming).
- 02:12 UTC: Platform alerts for increased 5xx from apigw.
- 02:13-02:22 UTC: Host-level CPU on sidecar processes climbs to 95%+; we see frequent GC stop-the-world pauses sampled by eBPF traces and python-timings (>250ms pauses).
- 02:18 UTC: API gateway begins returning 503s as health checks fail to get timely responses from sidecar endpoints; retries amplify backlog.
- 02:25 UTC: Autoscaler attempts to scale but new instances inherit warmed sidecar image with same workload; partial mitigation only after 02:50 UTC when traffic subsides.

Steps to reproduce (internal reproduction recipe):
1. In a dedicated us-west pool, spin up 200 concurrent streaming sessions pinned to the same sidecar binary.
2. Each session streams ~12KB/s tokens with intermittent small prompt transformations that allocate many small Python objects.
3. Observe sidecar CPU and GC pause durations via py-spy eBPF sampling.

Logs / Metrics (high-level links):
- Grafana: https://grafana.redwood/internal/d/streams/streaming-5xx?orgId=1&from=now-6h&to=now (pan to 2026-03-13 02:10)
- Loki: query 'job="session-sidecar" level=error "healthcheck"' for 02:10-03:00 UTC
- eBPF traces attached in investigation notes (link above Confluence page)

Investigation notes:
- The serving-runtime (GPU worker) remained responsive; crash dumps show no SIGSEGV or OOM on GPU processes.
- Primary failure vector identified in session-sidecar (Python) where GC induced stop-the-world pauses blocked the request dispatcher. Sidecar implements synchronous GC-unaware loops that block on large ephemeral lists during streaming transforms.
- Healthcheck endpoint handler is served by the same thread pool; during GC pauses HTTP handlers become unresponsive causing the API gateway to mark upstream as unhealthy and return 503s to clients.
- Amplification: API gateway retries queued requests to the same unhealthy host, increasing load and further lengthening GC pause windows (feedback loop).
- Correlated grafana panel: GC pause count track overlaps with 503 spike; p95 of sidecar request duration increased from 18ms -> 620ms during spike.

Immediate mitigations applied:
- 02:30 UTC: Support applied a soft-rate limit to LexiDocs tenant at the apigw layer (reduced concurrency by ~40%); error rate dropped but not to baseline.
- 02:38 UTC: Rolled a temporary probed-sidecar image with GC tuning flags (PYTHONMALLOC=malloc, PYTHONGC_THRESHOLD adjusted) to a small canary subset (3 nodes) — canary showed 60% reduction in pause frequency.
- 03:05 UTC: Manual routing removed the most impacted hosts from the pool until restart and GC-tuned image rollout completed.

Workarounds for customers:
- Ask customers to reduce concurrent streaming sessions or increase per-session batching where possible until a permanent fix is deployed.
- For LexiDocs we offered temporary request throttling and extended credits for the downtime window.

Root-cause hypothesis (current):
- Sidecar's streaming transformation path allocates many short-lived Python objects under heavy concurrency; default GC thresholds cause stop-the-world collection that blocks healthchecks. Combined with synchronous healthcheck handlers and apigw retry behavior this produced a positive feedback loop and 503 wave.

Permanent remediation plan (next steps):
1. Engineering (/runtime and /platform) to implement: move healthcheck endpoint to an isolated thread/process (non-blocking) OR make healthchecks serviced by the runtime side rather than the Python sidecar. Owner: Diego Morales. ETA: 2026-03-20.
2. Refactor streaming transforms to use allocation-pooling or to run CPU-bound transforms in a worker subprocess that can be restarted without failing healthchecks. Owner: Runtime team. ETA: 2026-04-02.
3. Add platform-level guardrail: apigw should detect upstream GC flapping via a new health metric (GC pause ratio) and avoid aggressive retry amplification. Owner: API Platform. ETA: 2026-03-27.
4. Add automated canary rollout of GC-tuned images for sidecars when rolling worker images to reduce blast radius. Owner: SRE. ETA: 2026-03-22.

Post-incident follow-up:
- Drafting a short postmortem; planned retro meeting with Runtime and API Platform on 2026-03-17.
- Open action items added to INT backlog for healthcheck isolation and GC-safe transform patterns.

title: Intermittent 429 bursts for multiplexed websocket chat causing degraded UX
preview: Issue summary: NimbusHealth is seeing intermittent bursts of HTTP 429 responses returned to multiplexed websocket clients on the hosted API during peak business hours. Impact: Users in their clinical triage chat are experiencing message failures and UI retries, increasing perceived latency and leading to dropped conversations. Scope: Affects ~10 client connection pools from NimbusHealth (enterprise account) in us-east region; seen across redwood-chat-small and medium models. Expected: Multiplexed websocket connections should see smoothing by token-level and connection-level rate limits with transparent backpressure, not sudden 429 bursts. Observed: Spikes of 429s lasting 20-40s every ~15 minutes, correlating with bursty fan-out from a single internal service. Steps to reproduce: 1) Establish multiplexed websocket session using shared API key for multiple browser clients (as customer implemented). 2) Simulate parallel message bursts (20 simultaneous requests over the same websocket multiplex) with messages that produce ~80-200 tokens. 3) Observe intermittent 429 responses returned with X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0 and Retry-After headers. Logs: Attached sample gateway headers and rate-limiter trace (see comment #2). Customer note: This started after migration to a client-side multiplexer used to reduce websocket count. Customer requests guidance on configuration to avoid disruption without dramatically lowering throughput.

title: Configuring sustained vs burst quota for multiplexed ingestion workers to avoid 429s
preview:
Issue summary: Customer Arcadia Labs reports recurring 429 throttles during steady-state ingestion runs from a fleet of short-lived uploader workers. They see 429s even when token usage looks within expected monthly/rolling quotas.

Impact: Intermittent failed uploads (10-25% of requests during 1–2 hour ingestion windows), delays in downstream indexing, and elevated retry traffic causing secondary waves of throttles. No billing disputes opened, but customer is concerned about reliability for nightly syncs.

Environment: self_serve tenant in prod (us-east primary, eu-west failover). Workers spawn ephemeral API keys (lifetime ~5–10 minutes) and open 20–50 concurrent connections per worker process. Ingestion pattern: steady sustained throughput ~120 requests/s for 90–120 minutes, with microbursts when workers restart.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Create N ephemeral keys and fan out 300 worker processes across 3 hosts.
2) Each worker initiates 20 parallel streaming sessions to /v1/generate for small prompts (avg 64 tokens).
3) Start all workers within a 90 second window (simulates a deployment rollout).
4) Observe 429 responses beginning ~30s after start; persists intermittently for the ingestion window.

Expected behavior: Smooth sustained throughput with occasional short-lived bursts handled by token bucket bursting.

Actual behavior: Tenant-level quota tokens are exhausted and 429s appear despite per-key buckets showing available tokens for some keys. 429s correlate with worker restarts and the fanout of ephemeral keys.

title: Edge probe split causes half-formed streamed messages after reconnect
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports that after an intermittent network disconnect, SSE streams resume but the first resumed payloads are half-formed JSON fragments and the client parser fails. Impact: Several NexaHealth production workflows ingesting streamed clinical summaries are producing invalid partial objects; customer reports ~15% of resumed sessions encounter parse errors and drops. Environment: prod, us-east, edge-proxy path through third-party CDN probe that occasionally inserts synthetic TCP probe packets. Observed behavior: On reconnect the server sends a resumed chunk that starts mid-UTF8 sequence and omits the JSON array opener; client logs "JSON parse error: unexpected token". Expected behavior: Stream resume should deliver complete, well-delimited framing and finalization events so clients can stitch without parse errors. Business impact: High — downstream ingestion pipeline discards incomplete records, causing missing clinical notes in processing.

Initial customer-provided details: NexaHealth observed issue at approx 2026-03-13T20:12:34Z during a regional CDN reroute. They captured client-side SDK logs and network capture showing a tiny probe RST/ACK/half-close followed by resumed SSE payloads with missing leading bytes.

Required next steps: Confirm whether edge-probe or load balancer is splitting TCP segments or injecting probes that cause the serving runtime's resume framing to emit a partial fragment; reproduce with controlled probe injection; add defensive guarding in the server resume path to ensure framing re-sync before emitting resumed payloads.

title: SSE TLS 0-RTT resumption during edge handoff produces partial markdown code fence and drops trailing list items
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer experiences truncated SSE streams when TLS 1.3 0-RTT (early data) is used and the client path migrates across edge nodes. The truncation consistently removes the closing markdown code fence (```), and drops the final 1-3 list items from enumerations. Impact:
- Customer's live documentation generator (NoteWave) shows malformed Markdown and missing bullet points in ~4% of streaming sessions during peak traffic.
- Affects user-facing web previews; breaks downstream parsers that expect closed code blocks.
Environment:
- Customer: NoteWave Labs (dedicated tenant)
- API: Hosted Redwood SSE endpoint via api.redwood.ai (prod-us-east)
- Model: gpt-redwood-3b-v1 pinned version 2026-02-25
- Network: client mobile app on LTE -> public mobile NAT -> CDN edge -> Redwood edge proxy -> serving pool
Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) Use mobile app with TLS 1.3 0-RTT enabled (client uses early data session resumption).
2) Start an SSE generation request that returns multi-paragraph Markdown with code fences and an ordered list at the end.
3) Trigger a short path migration by toggling mobile data (airplane mode on/off) or causing the client IP to change during streaming.
Observed behavior:
- Stream closes without an explicit end-of-stream event.
- Final code fence closing triple backticks are missing.
- Last 1-3 enumerated bullets are dropped.
- On reconnect the resumed stream may replay a short prefix but never retransmits the missing trailing tokens.
Reproducer used by Support/SRE:
- curl -N --http3 --resolve api.redwood.ai:443:34.201.10.7 
  -H 'Accept: text/event-stream' 
  -H 'Early-Data: 1' 
  --data-binary @payload.json https://api.redwood.ai/v1/streams/generate
- Observed same truncation when simulating sudden TCP teardown and SYN from new IP within 1s window.
Logs / traces:
- Trace IDs: trace-20260312-8b4a, trace-20260312-8b4b (attached redacted).
- Edge proxy (node-edge-7) shows connection close with TLS 0-RTT early_data_aborted at timestamp 2026-03-12T09:42:21.203Z.
- Serving runtime (worker r-14) forwarded stream with end-of-stream absent; internal stream offset reached but tail tokens not flushed to client.
Hypothesis:
- Early-data (0-RTT) session resumption interacts with connection handoff logic in the edge proxy: when a RESUME + path change occurs, the edge prematurely signals half-close without flushing buffered trailing chunks for streaming SSE sessions.
- There may be a mismatch between chunk boundaries used for prefix replay and finalization token emission when early-data is used (edge discards tail CRLF sequence that marks completion).
Temporary workaround provided to customer:
- Disable TLS 0-RTT / Early Data on the client (mobile) while we investigate. Customer reports immediate disappearance of the truncation after that change.
- As alternate mitigation, switch to short polling instead of SSE for critical flows until fix is deployed.
Severity and business impact assessment:
- Functional: User-facing content corruption for a subset of streaming sessions; parser-sensitive flows can fail.
- Business: Dedicated customer on reserved capacity; risk to renewal if not addressed.
- Classification: Sev1 (P1) for this customer due to data integrity impact.
Next steps / proposed action items:
1) SRE: instrument edge proxy to log early-data/0rtt handoff events with stream offsets and buffered chunk counts (actioned).
2) Dev: add defensive flush on handoff path to ensure trailing CRLF and close-token are emitted before half-close is propagated (investigation PR drafted).
3) QA: create deterministic test that simulates 0-RTT resume + path migration with SSE and checks for markdown closure.
4) Rollout: patch proxy in a canary region and validate with NoteWave test harness before global rollout.
Investigation notes summary:
- 2026-03-12 10:20Z: reproduced by SRE on staging using synthetic early-data client; confirmed missing final tokens.
- 2026-03-12 15:05Z: Eng noted that proxy code path for early-data uses a separate chunk buffer which wasn't being drained on connection rebind.
- 2026-03-13 08:40Z: Temporary client-side toggle confirmed as mitigation.
- 2026-03-13 12:50Z: PR #452 in realtime-proxy adds drain-on-handoff and a test.
Open questions for engineering:
- Does the same issue affect websocket-based streaming (client reported primarily SSE, but websockets may share close logic)?
- Are there any model/kernel-specific interactions (KV cache flush timing) that affect the final token emission?

title: Dedicated cluster: GPU contention causing sustained throughput collapse and autoscale oscillation
preview:
Issue summary:
Scanly Labs reports a sharp, sustained drop in throughput from their dedicated GPU pool in us-west starting 2026-03-11 09:12 UTC. Latency for streaming generation requests increases and many requests time out. The Redwood autoscaler began oscillating (rapid scale-up then scale-down) around the same time, making the throughput degradation persistent.

Impact:
- Production traffic for Scanly Labs' inference endpoints degraded: 60-80% drop in token throughput on pinned routes.
- Errors observed: tail latency spikes >10s, client-side timeouts, and a small increase in 5xx from serving-runtime.
- Customer-facing feature (real-time report generation) impacted for ~80% of requests routed to dedicated pool.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool: scanly-prod-pool-usw (reserved 12 GPUs across 3 nodes)
- Region: us-west
- Models: gptj-compat-13b (primary), redwood-rlhf-7b (fallback)

Observed behaviour / logs summary:
- Prometheus graphs show GPU utilization uneven: some GPUs pegged near 98% memory usage, others at 5-10% idle.
- Node exporter shows frequent GPU ECC/corrected error increases on node gpu-02-usw.
- Autoscaler logs: frequent scale events every 3-7 minutes triggered by queue growth, followed by immediate scale-down when some requests fail with GPU allocation error.
- Kubelet logs on affected nodes contain repeated container OOM and GPU allocation timeout messages.

Steps to reproduce (customer pattern):
1) Send steady production traffic to pinned model route for 30+ minutes (Scanly load test reproduced issue).
2) Observe queue length and latency grow, autoscaler adds capacity.
3) Some newly added nodes immediately show GPU allocation failures; scheduler reschedules pods causing node churn and eviction spikes.

Initial hypothesis:
- A single tenant process on one node is causing GPU memory fragmentation or a driver-level fault that prevents the scheduler from placing pods evenly, leading to practical GPU starvation on some nodes (noisy-tenant-like behaviour).
- Autoscaler is reacting to queue length but not accounting for allocation failures, so it oscillates and amplifies instability.
- Potential hardware/driver issue on gpu-02-usw (ECC counters, thermal events) contributing to allocation timeouts.

Customer's immediate ask:
- Restore stable throughput and stop autoscale oscillation.
- Short-term mitigation while root cause is identified.

References: slack #support-scanly (thread TS12345), infra PR 2345, Confluence runbook above.

3) Some newly added nodes immediately show GPU allocation failures; scheduler reschedules pods causing node churn and eviction spikes.

Initial hypothesis:
- A single tenant process on one node is causing GPU memory fragmentation or a driver-level fault that prevents the scheduler from placing pods evenly, leading to practical GPU starvation on some nodes (noisy-tenant-like behaviour).
- Autoscaler is reacting to queue length but not accounting for allocation failures, so it oscillates and amplifies instability.
- Potential hardware/driver issue on gpu-02-usw (ECC counters, thermal events) contributing to allocation timeouts.

Customer's immediate ask:
- Restore stable throughput and stop autoscale oscillation.
- Short-term mitigation while root cause is identified.

References: slack #support-scanly (thread TS12345), infra PR 2345, Confluence runbook above.

title: Streaming completions time out (504) when client-side HTTP/2 WINDOW_UPDATE is not sent by corporate proxy
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer AcmeFin reports repeated streaming completions from our hosted API returning 504s after long tails of silence. This appears only for some users behind a corporate network appliance.

Impact:
- ~30 requests/hr from AcmeFin experience 40-120s stalls then 504s when generating long completions (>512 tokens).
- End-user UX broken for interactive flows.
- Customer is enterprise tier; impact threatens a contract renewal conversation.

Environment:
- Prod edge (us-east) -> API Gateway (HTTP/2 enabled) -> NGINX sidecars -> serving-runtime GPU pool.
- Observed on models: gpt-open-13b, redwood-rl-7b.

Steps to reproduce (approx):
1) Customer client opens HTTP/2 connection to /v1/stream with streaming=true.
2) Server starts emitting many small DATA frames (KV-warmup + prefix output) over the stream for ~30-90s.
3) Corporate proxy between client and our edge silently fails to send WINDOW_UPDATE or stalls sending it back to our edge.
4) Our APIGW side hits server-side flow-control limits and begins buffering; write stalls accumulate; eventually the edge or NLB resets the connection resulting in 504/connection reset.

Observed behavior / evidence:
- tcpdump at edge (pid 2710) shows server DATA frames sent with no corresponding WINDOW_UPDATE from client side for ~65s (trace id f7a3...).
- APIGW metrics: per-request egress bytes continue but send queue grows; connection_write_latency spikes correlated with 504s.
- NGINX error log excerpt: "upstream timed out (110: Connection timed out) while sending request to upstream" at timestamps matching customer traces.
- No abnormal CPU/GPU load on serving nodes. Latency regression not seen for clients on consumer ISPs.

Hypothesis:
- The customer's corporate proxy (or a middlebox) implements a buggy HTTP/2 implementation that ignores or delays WINDOW_UPDATE frames under certain conditions (e.g., when TLS inspection is enabled). This prevents our edge from progressing flow-control, causing buffers to fill and the gateway to hit its connection idle/write timeouts. This is a network-level backpressure problem, not an application-level compute regression.

Investigation notes:
- Captured pcap at edge for two failed sessions and one successful session; comparison shows missing WINDOW_UPDATE frames only for failing sessions.
- Correlated with customer's proxy vendor (identified in support call) and their proxy logs showing HTTP/2 stream state transitions being held open for >60s.
- Reproduced internally by inserting a test proxy that delays WINDOW_UPDATE; identical behavior observed (write stalls -> 504).
- Confirmed sidecar NGINX configured to allow HTTP/2; keepalive and send timeouts default to 60s.

Workaround applied to customer (temporary):
1) Disabled HTTP/2 on the edge route for AcmeFin (fall back to HTTP/1.1), which eliminates the WINDOW_UPDATE negotiation and immediately resolved the 504s for their streaming requests.
2) Adjusted sidecar send_timeout to 120s for the customer route to reduce immediate resets while longer-term fix is evaluated.

Proposed long-term remediation options:
- Option A (preferred): Route affected customers to HTTP/1.1 on the edge when middlebox incompatibility is detected. Implement server-side detection heuristic for missing WINDOW_UPDATE patterns and auto-fallback.
- Option B: Introduce small server-side coalescing window: buffer small DATA frames up to X ms to reduce frequency of WINDOW_UPDATE reliance for proxies that mishandle fine-grained updates. Requires careful latency testing.
- Option C: Document recommended proxy settings for enterprise customers (TLS inspection exceptions or HTTP/2 compatibility modes) and include in onboarding checklist for Private/Dedicated sales.

Next steps assigned:
- Diego: roll out per-customer HTTP/2 fallback toggle and automation (P0 follow-up).
- SRE (L. Park): add metric/alert for "no WINDOW_UPDATE for >30s while server sending" to detect other affected customers.
- Support (Ava): coordinate with AcmeFin to engage their proxy vendor and share pcap evidence.

Status: mitigation applied (HTTP/2 disabled for AcmeFin), root cause strongly correlated to client-side proxy behavior, longer-term automation planned.

title: Payload-size throttle triggers upstream restart cycle and elevated 5xx for large embedding uploads
preview:
Issue summary: Customer BrightChat reports a sustained elevation in 5xx responses beginning 2026-03-12 02:10 UTC during large embedding multipart uploads. Impact: multiple clients seeing ~15-30% failed requests (5xx) for embedding endpoints; customer observed timeouts and client-side retry storms. Environment: prod us-east; self-serve tenant; burst of concurrent multipart uploads (~100 parallel multipart sessions from a single customer API key). Steps to reproduce:
1) Use BrightChat embedding bulk uploader with 100 parallel workers, each submitting multipart upload with ~12MB parts (total ~120MB per session).
2) Observe API gateway accept-queue growth and some requests truncated by body parser.
3) After sustained churn (~3–5 minutes), upstream serving-runtime processes restart (watchdog), causing 502/503/504 depending on stage.
Observed behavior: apigw emits 502->503 waves; logs show repeated body-parser WARNs about exceeding per-request buffer, then runtime processes OOM or watchdog-triggered restart. Keepalive socket counts increase (stuck half-closed), accept queue saturates and proxy starts dropping new connections. Expected behavior: large uploads should be gracefully rejected with 413 Payload Too Large or accepted and streamed to backend without triggering runtime restarts.
Key logs (excerpts):
- apigw: "body-parser: payload buffer exceeded; rejecting part" (2026-03-12T02:12:03Z)
- ingress-proxy: "accept_queue_len=512 -> 2048 in 30s" (2026-03-12T02:12:35Z)
- runtime: "watchdog: process pid=2345 killed due to vmem spike" (2026-03-12T02:13:10Z)
- kernel:dmesg shows oom_kill for python-worker pid 2345 at 02:13:12Z
Additional notes: customer uses a naïve uploader that sends large parts and performs retries without backoff, amplifying load. The issue appears to be a composite of apigw body-size buffer behavior, upload-service buffering strategy, and runtime memory handling for multipart streams.

title: WAF header rewrite causing subaccount quota attribution shift and unexpected 429s on private deployment
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer BrightCart (private deployment in eu-west) reported intermittent 429 throttles that started after a WAF policy change rolled out on their edge. The 429s are concentrated on a single subaccount and coincide with high background traffic from a third-party image-optimizer pipeline.

Impact:
- Several storefront ingestion workers began receiving 429s between 03:20-04:05 UTC on 2026-03-12, causing delayed catalog updates for ~12% of jobs.
- Customer reports intermittent user-facing slowness for product updates during the window.

Environment:
- Private deployment (customer-managed VPC) running Redwood private control plane (v1.8.3).
- Edge WAF sits in front of the customer ingress and applies custom header rewrites.

Observed behavior:
- Requests that previously attributed usage to distinct subaccounts have been attributed to a single subaccount id (subacct-9812) after the WAF rule change.
- Rate-limiter logs show token deductions against subacct-9812 for requests that include an X-Customer-Subacct header rewritten by the WAF into a header we use for subaccount mapping.

Expected behavior:
- WAF should not rewrite or drop the identity headers used for subaccount attribution, or the rewrite should preserve original values so per-subaccount quotas remain correct.

Notes:
- Customer rolled out a WAF rule to add an X-Forwarded-User header earlier the same morning. That rule also normalized Authorization headers for downstream routing. The normalization appears to have removed or replaced the original X-Customer-Subacct header in some flows.

title: Intermittent tail-drain timeouts for very large max_tokens / long-prefix streaming requests
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers are observing intermittent streaming disconnects and finalization timeouts when submitting requests with very large max_tokens (>=150k) and extended conversation history (many system/user messages). The generation runs for tens of seconds and then the client receives a 504 or streaming connection is closed without final chunk. This appears to correlate with sustained long-token drains and sudden KV cache thrashing on Dedicated pools.

Impact:
- Affected customer (NoteChat Inc., dedicated) reports ~10% of long-run streaming completions fail to finalize, causing truncated transcripts and user-visible errors.
- Business impact: production feature degradation for premium customers; customers are throttling long-context usage.

Environment:
- Dedicated GPU pool (g4dn-like), prod-us-east, isolated tenant.
- Requests routed to serving runtime version v1.48.0 with batching enabled and default cache policy.

Observed behavior:
- Streaming starts normally; progress chunks arrive for long periods. Around 30-90s into generation the stream stalls and then client sees a 504 or socket closed. Server logs show finalizer timeout and worker marked as overloaded then request requeued and dropped.

Expected behavior:
- Long-generation requests should stream to completion (within documented SLO) or be failed with graceful error that includes diagnostic id.

Logs / traces / evidence:
- Trace IDs: trace-us-east-20260310-7b3f, trace-us-east-20260311-c9a1.
- SRE dashboard: spike in finalizer-queue-time and kv-cache-evict rate between 03/10 11:12–11:30 UTC.
- Router logs show transient priority-queue backpressure and worker-level contention.

Steps to reproduce (customer repro):
1) Create a conversation with ~40 short turn messages (system + user tokens ~70k) as prefix.
2) Submit a single streaming generation with max_tokens set to 160000 and temperature 0.7 using model redwood-3b-quant.
3) Observe streaming chunks for ~20–60s, then either final chunk missing or 504 returned.

Workaround attempted by customer:
- Reducing max_tokens to 80k reduces occurrences to <1% but limits UX.
- Pinning to a non-quantized variant (llama-2-13b-instruct) changed failure mode to increased latency but fewer finalizer timeouts.
- Disabling client-side streaming and polling for full response caused same server-side timeout after ~2 minutes.

title: Catalog sync drift caused wrong model routing, quality regression, and enterprise customer escalation
preview:
Issue summary:
On 2026-03-10 at ~09:23 UTC we observed a large increase in customer-reported hallucinations and unrelated completions for Fresco Retail. Root cause traced to catalog-sync drift: the model-catalog service had an out-of-sync cache window and continued advertising an older (incompatible) variant for the 'product-description' route, causing requests to be routed to gptx-13b-v2-quant instead of the intended gptx-13b-v2 (non-quantized). This introduced repeated generation artifacts and a measurable quality regression for Fresco's production traffic.

Impact:
- Fresco Retail (enterprise) experienced elevated hallucination rate and semantic errors across catalog-enrichment requests for ~2 hours.
- API response quality SLO degraded (precision drop) and several customers opened escalations with support and account team.
- Billing exposure: some requests were served by a higher-cost quantized fallback path leading to a small invoice delta.

Environment:
- prod/us-east; Redwood hosted API; model-router + catalog-sync + serving-runtime in same region.
- Fresco's route: /v1/generate (route pinned to model alias 'fresco-prod:product-description').

Steps to reproduce (how support validated):
1) Replayed Fresco's sample request against the live router (timestamped trace id 20260310-0927-FR) -> observed model header set to gptx-13b-v2-quant.
2) Forced a catalog-sync refresh via admin endpoint -> subsequent requests routed to gptx-13b-v2 and quality returned to expected baseline.
3) Replayed the same prompt against both variants to compare outputs and token-level divergence.

Logs / Observations / Metrics:
- Catalog-sync service logs show an unsuccessful partial refresh at 09:17 UTC due to transient metadata store timeouts (backpressure). Cache TTL logic continued to serve stale entries until forced refresh at 11:30 UTC.
- Routing telemetry: model-mismatch rate spiked to 11% of fresco traffic between 09:20–11:20 UTC (baseline ~0.2%).
- Quality telemetry (prompt-eval harness): ROUGE-like and semantic similarity metrics dropped ~18% for sample prompt set.
- Billing meter reported ~3.6% of Fresco requests charged at quantized path unit pricing for the window.

Investigation notes (chronological):
- 09:25 UTC: Support received first Fresco alert; initial triage suspected quantized fallback due to GPU pool autoscale.
- 09:40 UTC: SRE ruled out GPU pool exhaustion; serving runtime healthy.
- 10:05 UTC: Instrumented model-router and catalog-sync. Found cache TTL decision had a race with incremental metadata updates (race introduced in recent change to TTL backoff).
- 10:45 UTC: Engineering reproduced drift locally by simulating metadata-store latency; confirmed wrong alias mapping persisted until manual refresh.
- 11:30 UTC: Manual catalog refresh + controlled rolling restart of catalog-sync workers restored correct routing.

Timeline (key events):
- 09:17 UTC: catalog-sync attempted refresh; metadata store returned 503 transiently.
- 09:20–11:20 UTC: increased model-mismatch traffic and customer complaints.
- 09:32 UTC: Fresco escalated to CSM and requested urgent remediation.
- 10:15 UTC: Support engaged SRE and Eng; incident declared P0.
- 11:30 UTC: manual refresh applied; routing corrected.
- 12:05 UTC: support reached out to Fresco with interim mitigation and credit offer.

Resolution:
- Immediate fix: forced catalog-sync refresh and rolling restart of catalog-sync workers to clear stale entries; added a short-term guard to prevent TTL-from-expiring-during-refresh behavior.
- Mitigated customer impact by routing Fresco traffic to pinned replica and issuing proactive credit for affected window.

Root cause:
- A recent change to catalog-sync backoff/TTL logic allowed a stale mapping to persist when the metadata store returned transient errors. The stale mapping pointed to an incompatible quantized variant for Fresco's route.

Actions taken during incident:
- Support escalated and coordinated cross-functional war room (Support, SRE, Serving Eng, Account Management).
- Manual refresh and restart restored correct routing.
- Customer notified with initial incident summary and remedial credits.

Post-incident analysis / follow-ups (see followup tasks):
- Engineering to back out/patch TTL-backoff logic and add stronger invariants (2026-03-12).
- Add automated canary test in catalog-sync to detect alias-to-variant regressions (2026-03-17).
- Add alerting for 'model-mismatch rate' anomalies per-customer with higher sensitivity (2026-03-15).
- Billing team to validate invoice delta and communicate adjusted invoice to Fresco (2026-03-14).

Customer communication summary:
- 11:50 UTC: Support sent Fresco a private update describing the mitigation steps and expected timeline.
- 12:05 UTC: Support offered a billing credit for the affected window and committed to a follow-up technical review.
- 2026-03-12: Post-incident call scheduled between Fresco Engineering and Redwood Serving Eng to review changes to routing safety nets.

Notes on risk / recurrence:
- The change that introduced the race has limited blast radius but can affect any enterprise customer with alias pinning when metadata store latency occurs.
- The short-term guard reduces probability but permanent fix required to prevent recurrence.

- Immediate fix: forced catalog-sync refresh and rolling restart of catalog-sync workers to clear stale entries; added a short-term guard to prevent TTL-from-expiring-during-refresh behavior.
- Mitigated customer impact by routing Fresco traffic to pinned replica and issuing proactive credit for affected window.

Root cause:
- A recent change to catalog-sync backoff/TTL logic allowed a stale mapping to persist when the metadata store returned transient errors. The stale mapping pointed to an incompatible quantized variant for Fresco's route.

Actions taken during incident:
- Support escalated and coordinated cross-functional war room (Support, SRE, Serving Eng, Account Management).
- Manual refresh and restart restored correct routing.
- Customer notified with initial incident summary and remedial credits.

Post-incident analysis / follow-ups (see followup tasks):
- Engineering to back out/patch TTL-backoff logic and add stronger invariants (2026-03-12).
- Add automated canary test in catalog-sync to detect alias-to-variant regressions (2026-03-17).
- Add alerting for 'model-mismatch rate' anomalies per-customer with higher sensitivity (2026-03-15).
- Billing team to validate invoice delta and communicate adjusted invoice to Fresco (2026-03-14).

Customer communication summary:
- 11:50 UTC: Support sent Fresco a private update describing the mitigation steps and expected timeline.
- 12:05 UTC: Support offered a billing credit for the affected window and committed to a follow-up technical review.
- 2026-03-12: Post-incident call scheduled between Fresco Engineering and Redwood Serving Eng to review changes to routing safety nets.

Notes on risk / recurrence:
- The change that introduced the race has limited blast radius but can affect any enterprise customer with alias pinning when metadata store latency occurs.
- The short-term guard reduces probability but permanent fix required to prevent recurrence.

title: Quota boundary and throttle-shepherding guidance for mixed bulk + realtime workload
preview:
Issue summary: Customer BeaconMaps seeing recurring 429s during daily bulk index refresh and low-latency routing for map tile annotations. Impact: Intermittent 429s causing failed embedders and delayed annotations in production (~18% of index refresh jobs fail).

Environment: Dedicated pool (dedicated-beacon-3), prod us-west. Heavy mixed workload: scheduled bulk embeddings (multipart uploads) + real-time annotation calls from mobile clients.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Trigger nightly bulk ingest for 80k documents via multipart upload with 16 parallel workers.
2) While ingest runs, send ~200 low-latency chat/annotation requests per second pinned to the same model variant.
3) Observe rate-limit headers and return codes.

Observed behavior: Throttles (429) occur predominantly on multipart upload endpoints and the streaming annotation calls report Retry-After of 5-7s. Customer reports that reducing parallelism to 4 workers removes 429s, but throughput drop is unacceptable.

Expected behavior: Segregated quota accounting or burst pools should allow short-lived low-latency calls to continue while bulk ingestion backfills without causing tenant-wide 429s.

Attached logs: API responses with headers, sample request traces, and telemetry timestamps provided in attachments.

Customer request: Guidance on configuring dedicated pool settings, recommended rate-window splits, and whether we can enable an isolated burst pool for long-lived multipart uploads without requiring migration to private deployment.

title: Prorated invoice line for async toolcall retry traffic not matching postback records
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports an unexpected prorated invoice line that appears to bill for ~18,000 toolcall invocations during a two-hour window on 2026-03-06. The customer asserts those invocations were retry traffic from a previous deploy and should have been tagged as test/heartbeat and therefore not billed.

Impact:
- Customer sees a +$1,250 prorated line on their 2026-03-06 provisional invoice.
- Customer is an enterprise Private deployment customer with reserved hours; unexpected charge impacts finance reconciliation and internal chargeback.

Environment:
- Peregrine Health private deployment (VPC) with hosted fallback enabled.
- Region: us-west-2.
- Affected model: redwood-gpt-large-v1 (toolcall-enabled variant).

Observed behavior:
- Invoice shows a prorated hosted-toolcall line; billing key points to hosted rate ID for toolcall invocations.
- Postback replays and reconciler logs show 0 accepted postbacks for production API key for the same timestamps; most events were marked as "retry-dropped" by the async-queue.

Expected behavior:
- Retry-dropped or test-tagged invocations should not be emitted as hosted postbacks and should not result in billed hosted-toolcall lines.

Steps to reproduce / investigation checklist performed by customer before filing:
1) Confirmed provisional invoice line present 2026-03-06 09:00-11:00 PDT.
2) Queried org postback exports for production key: zero successful postbacks in window.
3) Verified async-queue logs in their VPC showed repeated retry attempts with header X-Test-Traffic: true for a subset of connections.
4) Verified no canary alias was in use during time window.

Logs / attachments provided by customer:
- postback_export_2026-03-06.csv (attached)
- async-queue-retry-trace.txt (attached)

Requested outcome:
- Explain why invoice line was generated when reconciler shows no successful postbacks.
- If metering misattributed, request provisional credit and a root-cause explanation.

Notes for support: customer is escalated to account team; treat as high priority for billing reconciliation and temporary credit consideration.

title: Persistent 502/503 plateau from proxy TLS-handshake queueing and slow-starting upstream runtimes
preview:
Issue summary:
ScribeAI reported a persistent elevated 502/503 error rate starting 2026-03-12 02:10 UTC across multiple endpoints. Errors present as short-lived waves that plateau rather than sharp spikes — API gateway returning 502/503 while upstream runtimes report long TLS handshake queues and delayed worker start.

Impact:
- Customer-facing requests to ScribeAI (dedicated) experienced 10-20% error rate for ~3 hours during peak traffic windows.
- Streaming sessions intermittently failed to establish with 502s.
- Latency for successful requests increased by ~2x during plateau periods.

Environment:
- prod/us-east (dedicated pool for ScribeAI)
- Models: gptx-13b-v2 pinned variant
- Redwood stack: api-gateway -> ephemeral-proxy group -> serving-runtime (dedicated GPU pool)

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1. Generate steady small-payload traffic simulating ScribeAI client (60 rps).
2. Observe api-gateway logs: elevated upstream 502/503 responses, short-lived connection drops.
3. Correlate with ephemeral-proxy metrics: handshake_queue_depth spikes followed by slow worker spin-ups.

Observed behavior:
- TLS handshake queue depth increases to thousands of pending handshakes in the proxy layer.
- Ephemeral proxy threads report limited fd availability and per-thread accept backlog growth.
- Serving-runtime logs show a slow-start pattern for newly provisioned runtimes (delayed model init ~6-9s) leading to temporary capacity shortage and preemption events.
- api-gateway surfaces 502/503s (upstream reset / no healthy upstream) rather than 504s.

Expected behavior:
- Handshake queue remains low under normal concurrency; rapid accept/dispatch to runtime workers.
- Upstreams should be healthy or gracefully drain with fallback routing; no sustained 502/503 plateau.

Logs / metrics / traces (high level):
- api-gateway: errors: 502/503 rate jump at 02:08 UTC, sustained plateau until 05:30 UTC.
- ephemeral-proxy: handshake_queue_depth: 0 -> 3.2k at plateau; accept_queue_write_latency increased.
- runtime: worker_init_latency 6000-9000ms for new runtimes; repeated preemptions observed in autoscaler logs.
- netstat / kernel: no obvious ephemeral port exhaustion; socket TIME_WAIT in normal range.
- TCP/TLS traces indicate incomplete TLS handshake stages (server hello sent but no application data) corresponding with proxy accept backlog.

Immediate mitigation applied (by SRE):
- Temporarily raised ephemeral-proxy accept backlog and increased proxy thread pool by 25% to relieve handshake queue.
- Applied conservative rate-shed on low-priority tenants to prioritize dedicated customers.
- Enabled sticky routing to healthy warmed runtimes for ScribeAI to avoid cold-starts where possible.

Notes on customer communication: 
- Customer notified at 02:30 UTC that we were investigating TLS-handshake/backlog causes and applied temporary mitigations.
- Follow-up plan shared: deeper investigation into proxy accept handling, autoscaler slow-start tuning, and kernel tuning for accept queueing.

title: Cascading API-key handovers causing tenant concurrency exhaustion and intermittent 429 bursts
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports a burst of 429 responses across their tenant after an automated API key rotation during a high-throughput streaming upload window. The pattern is reproducible when their client rotates keys and simultaneously spawns short-lived prefetch workers that fan out requests to multiple routes.

Impact:
- LexaHealth observed ~30% request failures (429) for 7–12 minutes after an automated key rotation during a nightly data push.
- Business impact: ingestion pipeline stalled; downstream throughput SLA missed.

Steps to reproduce (provided by customer):
1) Configure two API keys (K-A primary, K-B rotated-in) with equal privileges under same tenant.
2) Client spawns 40 short-lived prefetch workers that request streaming completions and embeddings concurrently (burst over ~10s).
3) Client triggers automated rotation: start sending requests with both K-A and K-B for an overlap period of ~30s.
4) Observe 429s returned to many workers within 1–2 minutes of rotation, continuing for several minutes.

Observed behavior:
- 429 responses show rate-limiter reason in headers: X-RateLimit-Reason: concurrency_window_exhausted
- Token-bucket headers show remaining=0 for tenant-level bucket while per-key buckets occasionally show small positive values.
- Logs include many short-lived connection opens/terminates tied to rotated key handoff.

Expected behavior:
- Smooth handover with exponential backoff from clients should avoid tenant-level token exhaustion; short overlap should not trigger wide 429 storms.

Customer-provided artifacts:
- Upload link to request traces (redacted): s3://leixa-logs/rotation-2026-03-09/traces.tar.gz
- Curl examples and client pseudocode for rotation logic included in attachments.

Immediate ask:
- Guidance on mitigation steps to stop ongoing 429 bursts and recommended configuration changes for dedicated customers performing frequent key rotations.

title: Reserved pool throughput degradation caused by GPU power-capping + fragmented micro-batching
preview: Issue summary: Customer BeaconAI observed sustained p95/p99 latency regressions and intermittent 504 timeouts during peak windows on their dedicated reserved pool. Impact: Customer reports request timeouts and model responses taking 2x-4x expected time; production product is degrading for end users. Timeline: problem first reported 2026-03-10 08:10 UTC, support triaged and opened this ticket. Initial hypothesis included noisy-tenant and autoscaler undershoot. This ticket captures investigation showing a combination of node-level GPU power capping (thermal/clock throttling) combined with workload micro-batching fragmentation and a conservative autoscaler min_replicas setting.

title: Intermittent timeouts and doubled latency for repeated prompts: stale prefix / KV cache misses during streamed retry path
preview:
Issue summary: Customer (AtlasWrite, Dedicated) reports intermittent per-request latency spikes (1.8-2.5x baseline) and occasional streaming timeouts when users resubmit identical prompts or when client SDK retries a broken stream. Impact: Several customer-facing flows (autosummarize & chat history replay) experience user-facing slowdowns and ~5-10% request failures during peak traffic windows. Environment: prod/us-east, dedicated pool id d-pool-12. Observed primarily on gptx-13b-quantized and gptx-8b-finetune. Steps to reproduce: 1) Send a multi-turn conversation with a repeated prompt token prefix. 2) Force a transient stream disconnect on the client to trigger retry logic (SDK exponential backoff). 3) Observe server-side logs and latency metrics for repeated prompt ID. Expected behavior: Repeat prompt should hit warmed prefix/KV cache and return near-baseline latency with streaming retry preserving KV cache. Actual behavior: First retry shows KV cache miss, full KV lookup or cold fetch (~150-300ms extra) and in some cases the retry path times out after streaming proxy backpressure, producing a 504 to the caller. Recent change: Queryable prefix indexing refactor (serving-runtime v2.8.0) rolled 2026-03-08 suspected to coincide with regression window. Attachments: customer traces, sample request headers, and timing graphs uploaded to ticket attachments.

Logs / snippets: Error patterns observed in logs (prod us-east): "kv-cache: prefix-miss for key=prefix_0x4f2a" followed by "kv-fetch: cross-pool fetch latency=210ms" and then "streaming-proxy: retry-forward - client-side disconnect, initiating retry path". Several entries show the serving node evicting prefix entries after background compaction even when traffic indicates hot prefixes.

title: Partial-stream resume replay leads to duplicate external webhook side-effects for Python function-calls
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports duplicate external API calls (webhook POSTs) when using Python SDK streaming + function-calling. Behavior occurs after a network interruption causes the client to reconnect and resume a partially delivered streaming response. The resumed session appears to replay the function invocation without the original idempotency token reaching the downstream webhook, resulting in side-effect duplication (duplicate invoices / duplicate task creation).

Impact:
- Customer (SlateCRM Inc.) is observing duplicate third-party webhook side-effects impacting billing and downstream task systems.
- Occurs intermittently on long-running streaming interactions where the client reconnects to resume.
- Business impact: customer flagged as P1 due to operational impact on billing pipeline.

Environment:
- Redwood Dedicated (customer-owned VPC) in us-east region.
- SDK: python-sdk v1.8.7 pinned by the integration.
- Server: streaming-proxy v2.12.
- Webhook consumer is a third-party billing service that relies on a header idempotency-key to dedupe requests.

Desired outcome:
- Prevent duplicated external side-effects on stream resume by ensuring idempotency metadata persists across reconnect/resume flows, or provide a recommended workaround pattern for safe function execution from streaming clients.

title: Intermittent p99 timeouts after client stream restarts due to continuous-batcher linger accumulation
preview:
Issue summary: Customers (NimbleRetail dedicated tenancy) observed intermittent p99 user-facing timeouts (504 / request timeouts) concentrated after client-side stream restarts. Impact: Multiple production routes serving chat completions saw p95->p99 divergence, with p99 climbing from ~450ms baseline to 3-6s and occasional 504s. Affected customer reports started 2026-03-10 14:20 UTC and continued in waves.

Environment: prod (us-east), dedicated pool tenant for NimbleRetail, models: redwood-1.0-mk4 (default), fallback to gptj-14b configured.

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern):
1) Client opens a streaming connection and makes a short request (~12 tokens).
2) Client intentionally restarts the stream shortly after (~300-800ms) to refresh headers/session.
3) Server continuous-batcher observed many short-lived streams that resume quickly; linger timers accumulate pending micro-batches.
4) After a burst of restarts, batch flushes become interleaved and inflate processing latency for a subset of requests, producing p99 spikes and eventual client timeouts.

Observed behavior: Periodic clustering of very short-lived streams leads to "linger buildup" inside continuous-batcher. Instead of flushing quickly, micro-batches coalesce across restart boundaries and wait for stray tokens/kv-cache warmups, creating head-of-line blocking for otherwise short requests. Metrics show increased batch sizes for affected cohorts paired with elevated per-request scheduling delay.

Expected behavior: Short-lived resumed streams should either be routed to a fast-path (low-linger flush) or isolated so that stream restart churn does not elevate latency for unrelated short requests.

Logs/metrics:
- Request traces: https://grafana.redwood/company/d/streams/stream-traces?var-tenant=nimbleretail&from=2026-03-10T14:15:00Z&to=2026-03-10T15:30:00Z
- SLO alerts: #incidents in Slack: https://slack.redwood.com/archives/C05SUPPORT/p1646990000
- Example backend traces (zip): s3://redwood-support-dumps/SUP-135790/traces-2026-03-10.zip
- Notable log excerpt: "batcher: linger accumulated 42ms > configured20ms; flush delayed by kv-cache-warmup(1)" repeated across affected shards.

Investigation notes:
- Reproduced locally by running a client harness that repeatedly opens and closes streams with 200-700ms gap; showed same pattern of increased scheduling delay and larger-than-expected micro-batches.
- Continuous-batcher metrics show a drift in per-cohort linger window when there are many rapid stream lifecycle events; the cohort state machine was leaving stale timers active.
- KV-cache miss/warmup interactions: when a resumed stream is first routed to a node with cold KV cache, the batcher delays flush waiting for cache population which can exceed linger threshold for other coalesced requests.
- Autoscaling and GPU utilization looked healthy; not a capacity shortage.

Root-cause hypothesis: An interaction between stream lifecycle events (frequent restarts), cohort timer state, and kv-cache warmup causes the continuous-batcher to extend effective linger windows for a short period. That extended linger allows otherwise short requests to be coalesced into larger micro-batches or wait on cache-population, causing p99 latency spikes and some timeouts.

Immediate mitigations tested:
- Toggle: applied a config override for NimbleRetail tenant to reduce continuous-batcher linger max to 8ms (from 20ms) for affected routes; saw p99 drop back toward baseline in canary cluster.
- Enabled per-connection fast-path for short-lived streams (experimental) which bypasses cohort coalescing; reduced observed 504s by ~80% in smoke tests.

Next steps / action items:
1) Engineering: implement deterministic cohort timer reset on stream restart (owner: @Ethan Park, follow PR #2891). Target: revert lingering state immediately when a stream is closed/reopened.
2) SRE: roll out tenant config to all NimbleRetail routes and monitor for 24h (owner: @Marta Ruiz).
3) Product/Docs: add guidance for customers about safe stream reuse patterns and recommended client retry jitter (owner: @Aisha Khan).
4) Postmortem: schedule follow-up review with continuous-batcher owners and include workload patterns that trigger the state drift.

title: Edge failover + auth rotation causes missing metadata envelope and trailing paragraph silence on streaming resume
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports intermittent incomplete final metadata and a silent trailing paragraph when their websocket or SSE stream resumes after an edge failover/auth cookie rotation. The response body contains the content tokens but the final metadata envelope (terminal JSON object containing stream-level fields like 'id'/'usage'/'choices' metadata) is sometimes missing or cut and the last paragraph is not emitted, causing the client SDK to throw a parse error or appear to hang.

Impact:
- Several Inkwell Media users see a parse error or a UI spinner after a reconnect; last paragraph (summary) often never appears.
- Occurs ~0.5-1% of long-running streaming sessions for this customer during peak traffic.
- Affects critical editor flows where the final paragraph contains a license blurb.

Environment:
- Customer: Inkwell Media (dedicated account)
- Region: us-east
- Model: redwood-3b-v1 pinned
- Path: Streaming via websocket upgrade (wss) through edge-proxy cluster with header rewrite and cookie rotation set by customer-auth service
- Client: Browser webapp using Redwood JS SDK v1.4.2 behind corporate NAT/proxy

Steps to reproduce (attempted):
1) Start long-form streaming generation via websocket from the customer app with conversation payload > 800 tokens.
2) While streaming, trigger an auth cookie rotation (customer does so via SSO refresh) or simulate an edge failover by cycling one of the edge-proxy instances.
3) Client reconnects and requests resume using the session token; streaming continues but the final metadata object is missing or truncated and the closing paragraph is not emitted.

Observed behavior:
- Streaming tokens continue to arrive, and the last content tokens look complete, but no terminal metadata envelope (closing JSON with 'id' and aggregated usage) is present.
- Client SDK keeps waiting for the terminal event and does not surface the final paragraph. In some cases the SDK receives a small number of trailing bytes that appear to be a gzip fragment or partial header left by the edge-proxy layer, preventing parser completion.
- Reconnect sequences sometimes produce a duplicate initial system message, but not the terminal envelope.

Expected behavior:
- On resume, stream completes and the terminal metadata envelope is emitted exactly once; client SDK finalizes output and UI shows the concluding paragraph.

Logs / attachments provided by customer:
- 3 zipped websocket capture logs (wscat) showing trailing bytes on the final packet (attached to the ticket).
- Edge-proxy logs showing instance swap at 2026-03-10T17:12:34Z.
- SDK debug trace showing parser awaiting final 'end' event.

title: Dedicated pool throughput collapse from connection-reservoir leak causing batched-slot starvation
preview:
Issue summary:
DataScribe reported a sudden drop in dedicated pool throughput in us-west-2 starting 2026-03-10 14:10 UTC. Customer traffic experienced elevated p50/p95/p99 for batched routes; many short requests were queued and not scheduled to GPUs.

Impact:
- Customer-facing latency p99 increased from ~220ms to >2.8s for batched generation routes.
- Throughput dropped ~40% for the reserved pool attached to DataScribe's dedicated capacity.
- Automatic fallback to lower-tier variants triggered repeatedly, causing billing and quality regressions.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool: ds-prod-reserved-17 (us-west-2)
- Serving runtime: 2026.02.21-release
- Autoscaler: adaptive-v3
- Affected models: redwood-v1-13b (pinned), redwood-v2-7b (fallback)

Steps to reproduce (from customer):
1) Run high concurrency burst of many short chatlet streams (avg 120 tokens, 50ms client think-time) pinned to ds-prod-reserved-17.
2) Observe steady-state: throughput falls after ~20 minutes of sustained small-stream traffic; p99 climbs.

Observed behavior:
- Long-lived TCP connection handles and scheduler slots remained reserved even when requests idle between tokens.
- Scheduler shows high "connection_reservoir_in_use" metric approaching pool capacity; batchers unable to form optimal batches because slots appear held by idle connections.
- Autoscaler did not provision additional GPUs due to perceived capacity fullness; eviction signals low.

Expected behavior:
- Idle connections should yield reservoir slots after short idle window so batchers can reassign slots to active requests.

Relevant logs and dashboards:
- Loki query: {job=\"serving-scheduler\",pool=\"ds-prod-reserved-17\"} |~ \"reservoir\"
- Grafana dashboard: /d/pool/ds-prod-reserved-17/throughput-latency
- Trace sample: trace.redwood.ai/trace/8a3f2e7b

title: Erratic 5xx surge correlating with prefetch stall + connection-throttling on API gateway
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer (CloudNote Labs, dedicated) started seeing a sudden increase in 5xx responses for text-generation API calls beginning ~2026-03-10 01:18 UTC. Errors are intermittent but sustained in bursts over 10-30 minute windows.

Impact:
- ~18% of requests from the customer's endpoint returned 5xx during bursts, affecting production feature serving (user-facing).
- Visible increase in tail latency for streaming sessions.

Environment:
- Hosted: Dedicated pool for CloudNote (us-east region).
- Models: redwood/open-llama-7b pinned -> gptq-6b fallback configured.
- Traffic pattern: periodic prefetch-heavy workload (long prompts + multi-call prefetch).

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1) Run a sustained throughput test with prefetch_enabled=true and keep-alive connections from ~100 concurrent clients.
2) Observe API gateway connection throttling counters spike and upstream runtime 5xx rates rise.
3) Some streaming sessions drop with 502/503; non-streaming requests see 500/504.

Observed behavior:
- API gateway shows 'conn_throttle' metric jumps correlated to 5xx bursts.
- Runtime logs show short-lived worker exits with OOM/EAGER-EVICT warnings around same timestamps.
- KV-prefetch subsystem reports elevated cache eviction/repoll events (kv_prefetch_miss_rate).
- Autoscaler shows cooldown windows preventing immediate scale-up during initial burst.

Expected behavior:
- Stable 2xx success rate with graceful autoscaler scaling, no connection-level throttling for dedicated customers within committed capacity.

title: Streaming tool/function output tokens billed on final invoice despite console estimate showing zero
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports that streaming requests which include function/tool calls produced console estimates showing 0 tool-output tokens (console estimate column), but the finalized invoice contains token charges attributed to the tool outputs. Impact: LexiHealth reports a $9,200 unexpected charge on their monthly invoice tied to streamed sessions that used function calls for structured data retrieval. Environment: Production, us-east-1, enterprise account with reserved capacity and hosted fallback enabled. This affects streaming chat endpoints where the model returns tool invocations (function calls) and the tool outputs are streamed back as separate chunks.

Expected behavior: Console and estimate API should include a consistent token estimate for tool outputs (either zero if non-billable or the same token estimate used for invoice attribution). Final invoice should not introduce previously-unestimated token charges for tool outputs.

Actual behavior: Console estimate showed zero tool-output tokens for ~1.5M tokens across all tool invocations during the billing window. Final invoice shows ~1.45M billed tokens under a line labeled "tool-invoke-output" with reserved-rate fallback applied.

Customer impact: Revenue-impacting billing dispute, customer is holding renewal request pending resolution.

Attachments: Customer provided sample request IDs and screenshot of console estimate vs invoice lines.

title: Halogen Energy — fanout stall causing streaming silence, duplicate responses, and invoice exposure (post-incident follow-up)
preview: Issue summary: During a high-concurrency scheduled batch for Halogen Energy (enterprise, prod us-east), the request fanout path experienced a sustained stall resulting in partial streaming responses followed by duplicate completions. Impact: 11% of in-flight user sessions experienced either (a) a streaming silence lasting 6–18s before a replayed completion, or (b) duplicate completions delivered after client-side retry. Billing exposure: duplicate tokens were recorded for 0.8% of requests between 2026-03-10T09:42Z and 2026-03-10T10:26Z. User-visible effect: client-facing SDKs reported truncated transcripts and duplicated final messages; several Halogen automated workflows misinterpreted duplicates and triggered payment processing retrials. Environment: production, us-east, Dedicated pool: ded-pool-us-east-3, routing policy: capacity-first with multipath fanout to 3 backends. Time window: 2026-03-10T09:42Z — 2026-03-10T10:26Z. Steps to reproduce: 1) Replay customer traffic (provided capture) through multipath fanout; 2) Observe long tail in request-to-first-byte and duplicated completions when scheduler replays to alternate backend; 3) Reproduce with stress harness targeting redwood-xl-quantized at sequence lengths >800 tokens. Observed behavior: stalled mux in front of streaming worker pools (mux did not surface 5xx, but stopped forwarding chunks), scheduler retried to alternate node which completed the request again; dedup headers were inconsistently applied during the transient. Temporary mitigation performed: emergency rollback of a recent scheduler change (PR 427) and temporary reduction of fanout concurrency to 1 per request (via routing policy override).

title: session-kv-lease-sticky-causing-resumed-conversation-p99-latency-spike
preview:
Issue summary: ClearHealth reported a series of resumed conversational requests (user resumes session after short disconnect) that observe elevated tail latency (p99 ~4.2s) and occasional timeouts, compared to normal p99 ~250ms. This occurs after sessions have been inactive for ~30-90s. Impact: user-facing latency spikes and a small number of 504 timeouts causing degraded UX in production. Behavior is reproducible for certain session IDs and high-frequency resume patterns.

Context: This ticket was opened by Support after customer escalation. Initial triage indicates the serving runtime enters a blocking cold-path when a session key is considered "leased" by another backend shard due to a lease state that does not expire promptly. The behavior started after the kvcache lease change in release v2.18.
